# Git Submodule Documentation Pattern (LLM Guide)

## Concept
Isolate external dependency documentation and release-related documentation into reserved namespaces (using Git submodules), while allowing native, version-coupled project documentation to live directly in the `/docs` directory. This keeps the workspace clean and avoids path collisions.

## Directory Structure of `/docs`
Projects following this pattern structure their `/docs` directory as follows:
- `docs/` (Root / Arbitrary Subdirectories): Stores native, version-coupled project documentation (e.g., architecture, guides, API specs) directly in the main repository.
- `docs/lib-docs/` (Reserved): Contains read-only submodules for external dependency documentation (e.g. `docs/lib-docs/dependency-name`).
- `docs/releases/` (Reserved / Optional): Contains the project's own writable documentation/release repository as a submodule (not needed for end-consumer applications).

## Workflow

### Phase A: Setup in Library Repository (Write access)
1. Create a separate documentation repository (e.g., `my-library-docs`).
2. Add it as a writable submodule under `docs/releases`:
   `git submodule add git@github.com:my-organization/my-library-docs.git docs/releases`
3. Document features and tag documentation releases synchronously with library releases.

### Phase B: Consumption in Consumer Repository (Read access)
1. Add the docs repository as a read-only submodule under `docs/lib-docs/my-library`:
   `git submodule add git@github.com:my-organization/my-library-docs.git docs/lib-docs/my-library`
2. Fetch tags and pin to the exact version used in the project:
   ```bash
   cd docs/lib-docs/my-library
   git fetch --tags
   git checkout tags/v1.0.0
   ```
3. Commit the pinned submodule to the consumer repository.

## Benefits for AI Agents
Autonomously operating agents in the consumer workspace can scan `docs/lib-docs/` to immediately find version-accurate documentation without crawling code or external search engines.
